scenarios.prompt: I have two CSVs — orders and customers. Clean the column names, left-join them on customer_id, and reshape the result to one row per customer with total spend.
scenarios.expected_output: readr import, janitor::clean_names(), dplyr left_join + group_by/summarise, tidyr pivot as needed
expectations: Imports with readr and standardizes names (e.g. janitor::clean_names())
Aggregates to one row per customer with group_by()/summarise() (total spend)
Uses the base pipe |> and tidyverse verbs, not base subsetting

scenarios.prompt: This data frame has dates stored as 'MM/DD/YYYY' strings and a numeric column read in as character with stray commas. Parse both correctly.
scenarios.expected_output: lubridate date parsing + readr::parse_number / stringr cleanup inside mutate()
expectations: Parses the date column with lubridate (e.g. mdy())
Converts the character-numeric column (e.g. readr::parse_number() or stringr + as.numeric)
Applies the conversions inside a dplyr::mutate()

scenarios.prompt: Give me a missing-data overview of this data frame before I model it — which columns have NAs and how many.
scenarios.expected_output: per-column NA counts via dplyr::summarise(across()) or a tidy count, no silent imputation
expectations: Computes per-column missingness (e.g. summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.x)))) or equivalent)
Presents the result as a tidy summary
Does not silently impute or drop rows without flagging the choice

trigger_set.query: clean and reshape this messy data frame with dplyr and tidyr
left-join two tables and pivot the result to wide format
fit a linear regression and test whether the coefficient is significant
my dplyr pipeline is too slow on 80 million rows, make it faster
orchestrate this multi-step analysis as a reproducible targets pipeline
